channel.title: Graph Neural Networks on Chen Kai Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Graph Neural Networks on Chen Kai Blog

item.title: Essence of Linear Algebra (18): Frontiers and Summary
item.description: We have walked the long road of linear algebra together. We started with arrows in the plane and ended at the gates of quantum computers, the inner workings of large language models, and the topology of data clouds. The remarkable thing — the thing this series has tried to make visible — is that the same handful of ideas keeps coming back. A vector is a state. A matrix is a transformation. A decomposition is the structure hiding inside the transformation. A norm tells you when you can trust your computation. Once you internalise that loop, every “frontier” looks less like a foreign country and more like another dialect of a language you already speak.

item.title: PDE and ML (8): Reaction-Diffusion Systems and Graph Neural Networks
item.description:
What This Article Covers#

Stack 32 layers of GCN on a citation graph and accuracy collapses from 81 % to 20 %. Every node converges to the same vector. This is over-smoothing, the GNN equivalent of heat death — and the diagnosis comes straight from PDE theory. A GCN layer is one explicit-Euler step of the heat equation on a graph, and the heat equation has exactly one fixed point: the constant. The cure was published in 1952. Alan Turing showed that adding a reaction term to a diffusion equation can make a uniform state spontaneously break apart into stripes, spots, or labyrinths. The same trick — a learned reaction term — keeps deep GNNs alive.
